elements.label: Diann Shipione
elements.description: Pension Board Trustee and VP at UBS Financial Services. Primary whistleblower; raised concerns about MP-2 underfunding in 2002 and bond disclosure omissions in 2003. Faced retaliation from the board including ethics charges and attempted removal.

elements.label: Dick Murphy
elements.description: Mayor of San Diego 2000-2005. Presided over both the MP-1 legacy and the MP-2 approval. Re-elected in contentious 2004 race. Named one of three worst big-city mayors by Time magazine. Resigned April 25, 2005, effective July 15, 2005.

elements.label: Michael Uberuaga
elements.description: City Manager November 1997 - April 2004. Designed and implemented both underfunding agreements. Charged by SEC for securities fraud in 2008. Settled in 2010 with $25,000 penalty without admitting wrongdoing.
elements.notes: Settled SEC charges October 2010 without admitting or denying wrongdoing. $25,000 penalty.

elements.label: Edward Ryan
elements.description: City Auditor and Comptroller 1982 - February 2004. Signed letters falsely representing city's audited financials as accurate. Resigned February 2004. Charged by SEC in 2008; settled 2010 with $25,000 penalty.
elements.notes: Resigned February 2004. Settled SEC charges October 2010 with $25,000 penalty.

elements.label: Mary Vattimo
elements.description: City Treasurer. Participated in drafting false bond disclosure documents. Resigned May 2005. Settled SEC charges 2010 with $5,000 penalty.
elements.notes: Lowest penalty of the four settling officials — $5,000.

elements.label: Patricia Frazier
elements.description: Deputy City Manager of Finance. Revised false disclosure documents and signed closing letters for two bond offerings. Settled SEC charges 2010 with $25,000 penalty.

elements.label: Teresa Webster
elements.description: Former Assistant Auditor and Pension Board Member. Reviewed financials with false disclosures. Charged federally in 2006 (all charges dismissed April 2010). SEC case also dismissed August 2011.
elements.notes: Only official to have state, federal, and SEC charges all ultimately dismissed.

elements.label: Ron Saathoff
elements.description: Firefighters Union Local 145 president and SDCERS Pension Board Member. Received a special pension benefit allowing combined salary calculation, adding ~$25,000/year to his pension. Charged with 20 counts of fraud and conspiracy in 2006. All charges dismissed April 2010.
elements.role: Firefighter Union President / Pension Board Member
elements.notes: The special benefit designed for Saathoff was the core of the federal quid pro quo theory. Charged with 20 counts — the highest of any defendant.

elements.label: Cathy Lexin
elements.description: Director of Human Resources and Pension Board Member. Accused of designing the special pension benefit for Saathoff. Charged in 2006 with 16 counts of fraud and conspiracy. All charges dismissed April 2010.
elements.role: Director of Human Resources / Pension Board Member

elements.label: Larry Grissom
elements.description: SDCERS Pension Fund Administrator. Accused of devising special benefit for Saathoff. Charged in 2006 with 16 counts of fraud and conspiracy. Cleared April 2010.

elements.label: Lori Chapin
elements.description: Chief Pension Attorney for SDCERS. Accused of designing and concealing the special benefit for Saathoff. Charged in 2006 with 16 counts of fraud and conspiracy. Cleared April 2010 when honest services fraud statute was found unconstitutionally vague.

elements.label: Jerry Sanders
elements.description: Former San Diego Police Chief. Elected Mayor in November 2005 special election with 54% of the vote after Dick Murphy's resignation. Inherited the pension crisis and worked to address it.

elements.label: Michael Aguirre
elements.description: City Attorney elected in 2005. Took aggressive approach to pension crisis; attempted to overturn $900M in pension benefit increases. Seized SDCERS documents and took control of pension system's legal affairs in December 2005.
elements.notes: His efforts to overturn benefit increases were ultimately unsuccessful in court.

elements.label: Thomas J. Saiz
elements.description: CPA and principal of Calderon, Jaham & Osborn (CJO). Issued unqualified audit reports on bond offerings despite material omissions about pension obligations. Charged by SEC December 2007. Settled with $15,000 penalty.
elements.notes: Settled SEC charges without admitting or denying. $15,000 penalty.

elements.label: Bonnie Dumanis
elements.description: San Diego County District Attorney. Filed felony charges against six former and current city officials on May 17, 2005 under California Government Code 1090 (conflict-of-interest). Charges dismissed May 2010 after CA Supreme Court rejected the legal theory.

elements.label: Donna Frye
elements.description: San Diego City Council Member 2000-2010. Only council member to vote NO on the November 18, 2002 pension benefits vote after being warned by Diann Shipione. Later made a write-in bid for mayor in 2004.
elements.notes: Only council member to oppose MP-2 benefits vote.

elements.label: Scott Peters
elements.description: San Diego City Council President 2000-2008. Approved the 2002 pension pact as council president; voted for benefit increases under MP-2. Later defended himself as not originating the plan. Investigated findings labeled his role 'negligent' rather than criminally culpable.

elements.label: SDCERS
elements.description: San Diego City Employees' Retirement System. Established 1927. 13-member Board of Administration serves 20,000+ active and retired members. Site of the pension underfunding decisions and the quid pro quo benefit scheme.
elements.notes: Pension deficit grew to an estimated $2.5+ billion by end of scandal period.

elements.label: SEC
elements.description: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Investigated San Diego municipal bond disclosures 2004-2010. Sanctioned the City of San Diego (November 2006), charged independent auditor Saiz (December 2007), and charged five city officials (April 2008). First agency to secure financial penalties against city officials in a muni bond fraud case.
elements.notes: SEC case against Webster dismissed August 2011 — last action in the scandal.

elements.label: U.S. Attorney's Office (San Diego)
elements.description: Federal prosecution arm. Filed federal indictment January 2006 against five pension officials on fraud and conspiracy charges. Ultimately all charges dismissed April 2010 when honest services fraud statute was struck down as unconstitutionally vague.

elements.label: San Diego City Council
elements.description: San Diego's legislative body. Voted to approve Manager's Proposal 1 (1996) and Manager's Proposal 2 benefit increases (November 18, 2002). Only Donna Frye voted against MP-2. Collectively authorized the underfunding while increasing retirement benefits.

elements.label: Calderon, Jaham & Osborn (CJO)
elements.description: Independent accounting firm retained to audit San Diego's bond offerings. Issued unqualified audit reports on five bond offerings containing materially false information about pension obligations. Settled SEC charges.
elements.role: Independent auditor for city bond offerings

elements.label: Firefighters Union Local 145
elements.description: San Diego firefighters union. President Ron Saathoff sat on the SDCERS pension board and received a special tailored pension benefit as part of the MP-2 quid pro quo. His union support was critical to the pension board's approval of MP-2.

elements.label: Manager's Proposal 1 (1996)
elements.description: First pension underfunding agreement. Approved 1996 when SDCERS was 92.3% funded. Increased pension multiplier from 1.45% to 2%, created service credit purchase system, and established DROP (Deferred Retirement Option Plan). Set the structural precedent for underfunding.
elements.notes: System was 92.3% funded at time of approval — the last point of relative health before the crisis.

elements.label: MP-2 Pension Board Vote (July 2002)
elements.description: SDCERS pension board voted July 11, 2002 to approve the city's Manager's Proposal 2. Shipione raised concerns about the 'snake in the garden' accounting trick during the meeting. Ron Saathoff's vote was secured through the special benefit quid pro quo, per federal prosecutors.
elements.notes: Shipione's 'snake in the garden' phrase described a mechanism to reduce city contributions while increasing benefits.

elements.label: City Council MP-2 Vote (Nov 2002)
elements.description: San Diego City Council voted November 18, 2002 to approve the benefit increases under Manager's Proposal 2. Donna Frye cast the only dissenting vote after being warned by Diann Shipione. The vote locked in increased benefits while authorizing continued pension underfunding.

elements.label: Public Pension Disclosure (Jan 2004)
elements.description: On January 27, 2004, the City of San Diego publicly admitted errors and omissions in financial statements used in bond offerings. This disclosure triggered SEC and FBI/U.S. Attorney investigations.
elements.notes: The triggering event for all subsequent enforcement actions.

elements.label: S&P Bond Rating Withdrawal (Sept 2004)
elements.description: Standard & Poor's withdrew San Diego's AA-minus bond rating in September 2004 after the pension liability revelation. The city was effectively locked out of the municipal bond market for four years, unable to borrow for infrastructure.
elements.notes: The city earned the nickname 'Enron by the Sea' around this period.

elements.label: DA Files Felony Charges (May 2005)
elements.description: DA Bonnie Dumanis filed felony charges against six former and current city officials on May 17, 2005 under California Government Code 1090 (conflict-of-interest). All charges ultimately dismissed May 2010 after CA Supreme Court rejected the legal theory.

elements.label: Mayor Murphy Resignation (Apr 2005)
elements.description: Mayor Dick Murphy announced his resignation April 25, 2005, effective July 15, 2005, amid pension scandal and federal investigations. Time magazine had named him one of the nation's three worst big-city mayors.

elements.label: Federal Indictment (Jan 2006)
elements.description: Federal grand jury returned indictments against five pension officials in January 2006: Saathoff (20 counts), Lexin, Webster, Grissom, and Chapin (16 counts each) on fraud and conspiracy charges related to the Saathoff special benefit quid pro quo.

elements.label: SEC Order Sanctions City (Nov 2006)
elements.description: SEC entered an order in November 2006 finding that the City of San Diego committed securities fraud by failing to disclose pension and retiree health care funding problems in five bond offerings (2002-2003) that raised $260 million.

elements.label: SEC Charges Auditor Saiz (Dec 2007)
elements.description: SEC filed civil fraud action against Thomas J. Saiz and firm CJO on December 11, 2007 for issuing unqualified audit reports that failed GAAS standards and omitted material pension information. Settled with $15,000 penalty.

elements.label: SEC Charges Five Officials (Apr 2008)
elements.description: SEC charged five former San Diego city officials with securities fraud on April 7, 2008: Uberuaga, Ryan, Frazier, Vattimo, and Webster for participating in drafting and certifying false and misleading bond disclosure documents.
elements.notes: First time SEC charged individual city officials in a muni bond fraud case.

elements.label: SEC Settlement with Four Officials (Oct 2010)
elements.description: Four officials (Uberuaga, Ryan, Frazier, Vattimo) settled SEC securities fraud charges on October 27, 2010 without admitting or denying wrongdoing, paying a combined $80,000 in penalties. Historic first: financial penalties against city officials in municipal bond fraud.
elements.notes: Combined penalties totaled $80,000 — widely considered modest given the scale of the fraud.

elements.label: Federal Criminal Charges Dismissed (Apr 2010)
elements.description: U.S. District Judge Roger Benitez dismissed all federal criminal charges against the five pension officials on April 8, 2010, ruling the honest services fraud statute was unconstitutionally vague and failed to give fair warning that the defendants' conduct could constitute federal fraud.
elements.notes: U.S. Supreme Court later affirmed in Skilling v. United States (June 2010) that the honest services statute was too vague.

elements.label: S&P Reinstates Bond Rating (2008)
elements.description: Standard & Poor's reinstated San Diego's bond rating in 2008 at A with positive outlook, ending the city's four-year exclusion from the public bond markets.
elements.notes: Rating reinstated at A, lower than the pre-scandal AA-minus.

connections.label: warned about MP-2 fraud
connections.description: Shipione raised concerns to city council members about the underfunding proposal in 2002, calling it 'possibly corrupt.' Her warning was ignored by all members except Donna Frye.

connections.label: alerted mayor to underfunding scheme
connections.description: Shipione raised concerns about the pension underfunding proposal to Mayor Murphy's administration. Her warnings were not acted upon.

connections.label: sole dissenting vote
connections.description: Only city council member to vote NO on the November 18, 2002 pension benefit increases after being warned by Diann Shipione.

connections.label: approved as council president
connections.description: Peters, as city council president, voted to approve the MP-2 benefit increases. Later argued he did not originate the plan.
connections.notes: Investigators later characterized his role as 'negligent' rather than criminally culpable.

connections.label: inherited and expanded underfunding structure
connections.description: Uberuaga inherited the MP-1 framework and architected the MP-2 underfunding deal, which compounded the existing pension deficit while granting further benefit increases.

connections.label: designed and proposed MP-2
connections.description: Uberuaga as City Manager designed Manager's Proposal 2 and brought it to the pension board and city council for approval.

connections.label: cast decisive vote; received special benefit
connections.description: Saathoff voted to approve MP-2 on the pension board. Federal prosecutors alleged his vote was secured through a quid pro quo: a special pension benefit allowing combined salary calculation, worth ~$25,000/year more.
connections.notes: Classified as 'alleged' because federal charges were dismissed. The vote is verified; the quid pro quo framing is the prosecution's theory.

connections.label: designed special pension benefit
connections.description: Lexin was accused of designing the special pension benefit for Saathoff that allowed him to combine salaries when calculating his pension, increasing it by ~$25,000/year. Federal charges dismissed 2010.

connections.label: co-devised special pension benefit
connections.description: Grissom, as SDCERS administrator, was accused of devising the special benefit for Saathoff in coordination with Lexin, Webster, and Chapin.

connections.label: designed and concealed special benefit
connections.description: Chapin, as SDCERS chief pension attorney, was accused of designing and legally concealing the special pension benefit for Saathoff.

connections.label: union president
connections.description: Saathoff was president of Firefighters Union Local 145 while simultaneously serving as a pension board trustee — the dual role at the heart of the conflict-of-interest.

connections.label: signed false audit certifications
connections.description: Ryan signed letters falsely representing that the city's audited financials in the bond offerings were accurate, omitting the pension underfunding crisis.

connections.label: drafted false bond disclosure documents
connections.description: Vattimo participated in drafting the misleading bond offering documents that omitted the pension funding crisis.

connections.label: revised and signed false disclosure documents
connections.description: Frazier revised the bond disclosure documents and signed closing letters for two bond offerings that omitted material pension information.

connections.label: issued unqualified audits despite material omissions
connections.description: CJO issued unqualified audit reports on bond offerings despite failing to comply with GAAS and failing to identify the material omissions about pension obligations.

connections.label: principal of firm
connections.description: Saiz was the principal responsible for conducting the city audits at CJO.

connections.label: triggered bond rating withdrawal
connections.description: The January 2004 public disclosure of pension omissions led S&P to withdraw the city's AA-minus bond rating by September 2004.

connections.label: issued securities fraud order against city
connections.description: SEC formally sanctioned the City of San Diego for securities fraud in municipal bond offerings that raised $260 million based on false and misleading disclosures.

connections.label: charged independent auditor with fraud
connections.description: SEC charged Thomas Saiz and CJO with fraud for issuing unqualified audit reports that enabled the false bond disclosures.

connections.label: charged five officials with securities fraud
connections.description: SEC charged Uberuaga, Ryan, Frazier, Vattimo, and Webster with securities fraud for participating in drafting and certifying false bond disclosures.

connections.label: resolved via settlement
connections.description: Four of five SEC defendants settled their charges in October 2010, paying a combined $80,000 in penalties without admitting or denying wrongdoing.

connections.label: filed federal fraud indictments
connections.description: U.S. Attorney's Office secured federal grand jury indictments against five pension officials on fraud and conspiracy charges related to the Saathoff benefit quid pro quo.

connections.label: dismissed — statute unconstitutionally vague
connections.description: All federal criminal charges against the five pension officials were dismissed when Judge Benitez ruled the honest services fraud statute was unconstitutionally vague.

connections.label: filed state felony charges
connections.description: DA Dumanis filed state felony charges under California Gov Code 1090 against six officials for conflict-of-interest in the pension benefit deals.

connections.label: resigned amid scandal
connections.description: Murphy announced his resignation on April 25, 2005, directly attributable to the pension scandal and federal investigations into the city.

connections.label: succeeded as mayor
connections.description: Sanders was elected in the November 2005 special election to succeed Murphy and inherited the pension crisis.

connections.label: attempted to overturn illegal benefits
connections.description: City Attorney Aguirre took control of SDCERS's legal affairs in December 2005, seized documents, and attempted to invalidate $900M in pension benefit increases, ultimately without success.

connections.label: structural precedent for second underfunding deal
connections.description: MP-1's benefit increases and underfunding mechanism created the fiscal conditions that led to MP-2 — the fund fell below required levels, prompting a second underfunding agreement rather than corrective action.

connections.label: approved by board; forwarded to council
connections.description: After the pension board voted to approve MP-2 in July 2002, the City Council voted to ratify and implement the benefit increases in November 2002.

connections.label: created ~$1.35B unfunded liability
connections.description: The combined effect of MP-1 and MP-2 created an unfunded pension liability estimated at $1.35-2.2 billion that was eventually disclosed in January 2004.

connections.label: four-year bond market exclusion
connections.description: S&P's 2004 rating withdrawal effectively locked San Diego out of the bond market for four years. The 2008 reinstatement came at a lower rating (A vs. former AA-minus).
